name: Low-Cheese Marinara Pizza
description: A classic marinara pizza for a low-cheese eater: the same homemade dough from pizza night under crushed San Marzano tomato, garlic, oregano, and basil, with just a light dusting of parmesan (or none). Dough adapted from How Sweet Eats' pizza; marinara is the classic Neapolitan style.
recipeIngredient: 1 cup crushed San Marzano tomatoes (or good marinara)
2-3 tablespoons grated parmesan (light — or omit for no cheese)

recipeInstructions.text: Make the dough: stir the yeast and honey into the lukewarm water; let sit until foamy, ~5 minutes. Mix in the flour and salt to form a dough, knead briefly, cover, and let rise until puffy, about 1 hour.
Preheat the oven as hot as it goes (500°F+) with a stone or steel if you have one.
Stir the grated garlic, olive oil, and oregano into the crushed tomatoes; season with a little salt.

Stretch the dough thin on a floured surface or parchment. Spread a thin layer of the tomato sauce, leaving a 1/2-inch border. Add red pepper flakes if using and only a light dusting of parmesan (or none).
Bake until the crust is browned and crisp, 12 to 15 minutes. Finish with torn fresh basil and a drizzle of olive oil.

author: Adapted (dough: How Sweet Eats)

ai.cliffNotes: A bright, barely-cheesy marinara pizza on pizza-night dough — tomato, garlic, oregano, basil, with just a whisper of parmesan (or none).
keyTips: Use a thin layer of sauce and go light on (or skip) the cheese — a wet, loaded pie steams the crust soft; marinara is meant to be lean and crisp.
Grate the garlic raw into the sauce rather than cooking it first; the quick bake mellows it and keeps it bright.
Stretch thin and bake on the hottest setting with a preheated stone/steel for the crispest base.
Finish basil and a drizzle of good olive oil AFTER baking so they stay fresh and fragrant.
